title-group.article-title: Box C/D snoRNPs and MDT-15/MED15 regulate mitochondrial surveillance via fatty acid metabolism

article-meta.aff: 1Department of BioSciences, Rice University, 6100 Main St, MS140, Houston, TX, 77005, USA

author-notes.corresp: #Corresponding author: Department of BioSciences, Rice University, 6100 Main St, MS140, Houston, TX, 77005, USA. kirienko@rice.edu

license.license-p: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, which allows reusers to distribute, remix, adapt, and build upon the material in any medium or format for noncommercial purposes only, and only so long as attribution is given to the creator.

abstract.p: Multicellular organisms constantly encounter biotic and abiotic stressors that threaten to disrupt their homeostasis. To counteract this, they have evolved a series of conserved defense networks which monitor organellar function and the accumulation of aberrant molecular signals. As mitochondrial dysfunction can lead to a variety of pathologies, including metabolic impairment, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and sensitivity to bacterial infections, understanding these mitochondrial surveillance pathways is crucial.

Previous research in our lab established that one such pathway, the Ethanol Stress and Response Element (ESRE), was activated by a broad range of stresses, including exposure to the Gram-negative, opportunistic human pathogen Pseudomonas aeruginosa. In this report we show that MDT-15 and its downstream effectors, fatty acid desaturases FAT-6 and FAT-7, were required for ESRE activation. Interestingly, supplementation with fatty acids downstream of FAT-6/FAT-7 activity, from C. elegans’ PUFA pathway, rescued ESRE activation in their knockdowns. Additionally, disruption of mdt-15, or its downstream effector fat-6 by RNAi diminished worm survival during exposure to P. aeruginosa, indicating that these genes play a role in host defense. Notably, box C/D snoRNPs complex that we previously identified as an activator of ESRE, is required for upregulation of fatty acid metabolism under ESRE-activating conditions. Our results provide insight into a novel interplay between box C/D snoRNPs, lipids, and MDT-15/Mediator in the regulation of mitochondrial surveillance and innate immunity.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Mitochondria have long been characterized as “the powerhouse of the cell”. While this oversimplification has some truth, these organelles also play key roles in regulating a variety of important biochemical processes that determine cellular health, which, if compromised, can lead to metabolic and cardiovascular disease, neurodegeneration, cancer, and aging (1). To maintain mitochondrial homeostasis, multicellular organisms have developed surveillance mechanisms and pathways that monitor a wide variety of signals, including mitochondrial bioenergetics, proteostasis, and key metabolites for disruptions that may indicate imbalanced homeostasis. In response to these signals, the cell activates gene expression programs that are coordinated between mitochondria and the nucleus, which are intended to regain a more normal function (2).

The most studied of these pathways is the mitochondrial unfolded protein response (UPRmt) (3). This pathway activates the transcription factor ATFS-1, which upregulates mitochondrial chaperone effectors in response to mitochondrial dysfunction. The PINK1/Parkin pathway is responsible for autophagic degradation of mitochondria and selective respiratory chain turnover, which can be active in basal or stressed conditions (4,5). Another mitochondrial surveillance system is the Mitogen-activated protein kinase cascade (MAPKmt), defined by DLK-1/SEK-3/PMK-3 signaling cascade, which responds to mitochondrial bioenergetic abnormalities and plays a vital role in lifespan (6). As reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a common byproduct of mitochondrial function and play an important role in aging, levels of mitochondrial ROS are monitored via the Ethanol and Stress Response Element (ESRE) surveillance pathway (7).

The role of mitochondria in innate immunity has also been established, specifically in the context of host-pathogen interactions. It has been shown that activation of UPRmt provides resistance to agar-based infections with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a Gram-negative opportunistic human pathogen commonly associated with hospital-acquired infections (8,9). Our lab has also established the importance of the ESRE pathway in host defense against P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis or acute iron removal (10).

Recently, there has been an increased appreciation for the diverse role of lipid metabolism in other cellular processes besides structural integrity, storage, energy production, aging, and the regulation of proliferation (11,12). Previously, it was demonstrated that monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFAs) play a role in innate immune activation in Caenorhabditis elegans (13). Additionally, the role of mitochondria in linking energy metabolism and innate immune activation has been highlighted through lipids such as ceramides, which can provide protection from toxin- or pathogen-induced mitochondrial damage (14).

Another interesting component that has been linked to immunometabolism are small nucleolar ribonucleoproteins (snoRNPs). Small non-coding RNAs (snoRNAs) in complex with snoRNPs guide nucleotide modification of ribosomal RNAs (rRNAs) or small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs) (15,16). SnoRNPs are divided into two groups: those associated with box C/D or box H/ACA RNAs, which modify rRNA by performing 2’-O-methylation or pseudouridylation, respectively (17,18). A non-canonical role for snoRNPs was previously shown, where in response to doxorubicin-induced oxidative stress, Rpl13a (box C/D family) snoRNPs accumulate in the cytoplasm (19). Additionally, Rpl13a snoRNPs have been shown to regulate the response to lipotoxic and oxidative stress (20–22). Box C/D and box H/ACA snoRNAs have also been linked to innate immune activation, specifically by activating the protein kinase response (PKR), which can respond to dsRNA and metabolic stress (21,23). Previous research in our lab also identified a role for box C/D snoRNPs in regulation of the ESRE mitochondrial surveillance pathway (24).

Kwon et al. previously demonstrated the presence of a conserved, 11-nucleotide DNA element (TCTGCGTCTCT) known as the ESRE motif in genes upregulated in response to ethanol-induced stress (Fig. 1A; note that all motifs shown in Fig. 1 were derived de novo from the gene lists in relevant publications using MEME) (25). In 2017, Tjahjono and Kirienko identified the same ESRE motif in genes upregulated in response to liquid-based pathogenesis with P. aeruginosa (LK-Pa), or acute iron removal, indicating the importance of ESRE in host defense (Fig. 1B) (10). Follow-up research by Tjahjono et al. linked the ESRE pathway to mitochondrial surveillance, specifically to sensing increased ROS from aberrations in the mitochondrial electron transport chain (7).

C. elegans is a widely used model nematode, with several attractive features for studying innate immunity or surveillance pathways. Because it has been used in many subdisciplines of biology, it is very well understood and has many favorable characteristics, including incredibly well-studied development, neurology, aging, and infectious diseases (26,27). It has some of the best genetic tools of any model organism. Its small size and short generation time facilitate high-throughput experiments and substantial automation, which provide robust data (26). Its innate immune system shows significant conservation with a broad variety of other metazoans, including mammals, resulting in general applicability of discoveries about host-pathogen interactions (28,29). Finally, C. elegans is naturally a bacterivore. In most cases, it can be infected by merely feeding it a pathogen of interest. Additionally, since ingestion and infection are natural processes, it has evolved robust self-defense pathways to limit bacterial infection (28).

In this study, we identify the role of fatty acids and box C/D snoRNPs in mitochondrial surveillance activation and host defense. The evolutionarily-conserved mediator subunit MDT-15/MED15, which is a major regulator of lipid metabolic processes, and its downstream effectors FAT-6 and FAT-7 were required for mitochondrial ESRE, UPRmt, and MAPKmt activation. Additionally, we demonstrated that fatty acids from C. elegans’ polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) synthesis pathway are indispensable specifically for ESRE but not for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation. Furthermore, we show that MDT-15 and FAT-6 are necessary for host defense against P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis, in a PMK-1-independent manner. Moreover, we reveal a non-canonical role for snoRNPs in the regulation of fatty acid metabolism through FAT-7. Finally, we demonstrate a regulation of MDT-15 by box C/D snoRNPs as well as regulation of box C/D snoRNPs by MDT-15.

sec.title: The evolutionarily conserved mediator subunit MDT-15/MED15 is required for ESRE activation
sec.p: While investigating the biology underlying the host response to P. aeruginosa in Liquid Killing, we previously carried out a high-throughput screen to identify small molecules that increase C. elegans survival. Five of the hits we identified showed signs of stimulating host innate immunity, with LK56 providing the most protection (30). Although transcriptional profiling was performed on LK56-treated worms, and the compound was shown to depend on MDT-15/MED15 for its activity, a clear mechanism of action was not identified.

To uncover the potential mechanism of LK56-mediated rescue, we analyzed upstream regulatory sequences from genes upregulated by LK56 using RSAT (31) and MEME (32). Analyses revealed significant enrichment of the ESRE motif (30.7%) in their promoters (Fig. 1C). Similar sequence analyses of genes differentially regulated by LK34 and LK38 also showed enrichment for the ESRE motif, albeit at lower rates (22.6% and 19.8%, respectively, compared to ~8% of the genome as a whole) (Table S1 and S2). Gene ontology analysis of LK56-upregulated genes using WormCat (33) found enrichment of genes involved in lipid metabolism, detoxification, and pathogen response (Fig. 1D). This was consistent with previous research in the Kirienko lab showing that LK56 provided increased resistance to Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterococcus faecalis, and Staphylococcus aureus (30). Since LK56-responsive genes exhibited enrichment for the ESRE motif, and LK56 activity depends on MDT-15, we hypothesized that activation of ESRE genes may similarly rely on MDT-15. To test this, RNAi was used to knock down mdt-15 in a C. elegans strain carrying a GFP reporter driven by three tandem repeats of the ESRE motif (3XESRE::GFP). This reporter strain was exposed to rotenone, which is known to activate ESRE (10). GFP expression was virtually abolished on mdt-15(RNAi), even in the presence of rotenone (Fig. 1E–F). These results indicate that the evolutionarily-conserved mediator subunit MDT-15 is required for ESRE activation in response to mitochondrial damage.

sec.title: FAT6 and FAT-7 link ESRE activation to lipid metabolism
sec.p: Amongst its many targets, MDT-15/MED15 has previously been shown to regulate the expression of the Δ9-fatty acid desaturases FAT-6 and FAT-7 (34). These proteins convert the saturated stearic acid (SA) into monounsaturated oleic acid (OA) (Fig. 2A), with the latter being involved in innate immunity (13). Desaturation of SA is required for proper membrane function, and MUFAs play key roles in lipid distribution, fat storage, and signaling (35). For this reason, we tested the effect of RNAi targeting fat-6 and fat-7 on GFP expression in rotenone-treated 3XESRE::GFP worms (Fig. 2B–C). Similar to MDT-15, FAT-6 and FAT-7 were required for full activation of the ESRE reporter. This effect was also observed for the expression of native ESRE genes using qRT-PCR (Fig. 2D), confirming that MUFA production is necessary for activation of mitochondrial surveillance.

Multicellular organisms have numerous mitochondrial surveillance pathways, responding to a variety of stresses (2). We tested whether MDT-15, FAT-6, and FAT-7 also regulate the mitochondrial unfolded protein response (UPRmt) or the mitochondrial MAPK cascade (MAPKmt). RNAi was used to knock down mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 in reporter strains carrying Phsp-6::GFP (UPRmt) or Ptbb-6::GFP (MAPKmt) that were activated by spg-7(RNAi) (Fig. 3A–B). All three genes were required for activation of UPRmt, while only mdt-15 and fat-6 were required for MAPKmt activation (Fig. 3C–D). The decrease in reporter activation for either pathway after targeting fatty acid metabolism was significantly less than for ESRE activation (Fig. S1). Interestingly, activation of the MAPKmt pathway by mdt-15(RNAi), fat-6(RNAi), or fat-7(RNAi) under basal conditions was comparable to spg-7(RNAi) induction (Fig. 3E–F), indicating significant differences in the function of this pathway in mitochondrial surveillance programs.

sec.title: Oleic acid plays a role in ESRE pathway activation
sec.p: FAT-6 and FAT-7 are known to be involved in OA production and innate immune activation (13). To determine whether OA production is sufficient to rescue ESRE activation, RNAi was used to knock down mdt-15, fat-6, and fat-7 in the 3XESRE::GFP reporter reared on media containing 500 μM OA (Fig. 4A–C). Similar results were seen regardless of whether the non-ionic surfactant Tergitol (NP-40) was added to the medium (compare Fig. S2A–C and Fig. 4B–D). Supplementation of OA to the treatment enhanced rotenone-triggered ESRE activation when fat-6 or fat-7, but not mdt-15, were knocked down by RNAi (Fig. 4B–D). Interestingly, OA supplementation enhanced rotenone-triggered ESRE activation in worms reared on the empty RNAi vector, suggesting that OA limitation normally curtails ESRE mitochondrial surveillance even when lipid metabolism is not disrupted. However, in the absence of mitochondrial stress triggered by rotenone, OA was not sufficient to induce ESRE pathway activation (Fig. S3), indicating that OA augments activation under stress.

sec.title: Fatty acids downstream of oleic acid are required for mitochondrial ESRE activation
sec.p: The rescue of the reduction in rotenone-mediated GFP expression in fat-6(RNAi) and fat-7(RNAi) by OA indicated that it functions downstream of or in parallel to these genes. To examine the role of downstream fatty acids in the C. elegans PUFA pathway (Fig. 5A) in mitochondrial surveillance, the 3XESRE::GFP reporter strain was reared on media containing 500 μM linoleic acid (LA), 500 μM γ-linolenic acid (GLA), or 500 μM arachidonic acid (ARA), and mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 were knocked down using RNAi. ESRE expression was then measured after rotenone induction at different time points (Fig. 5B–D, Fig. S4A–F). Supplementation with any of these fatty acids improved ESRE expression after rotenone exposure. For this reason, we also examined rotenone-mediated expression of native ESRE genes during ARA supplementation (Fig. 5E). Expression of these genes was upregulated, indicating the importance of fatty acids in ESRE effector gene activation.

As mdt-15 and fat-6 were involved in spg-7(RNAi)-mediated activation of UPRmt and MAPKmt, we tested whether supplementation with these fatty acids would rescue activation of these reporters after mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 were knocked down. In contrast to observations from the ESRE reporter, OA, LA, or ARA supplementation did not increase spg-7(RNAi)-mediated UPRmt reporter expression (Fig. S5–S7). In contrast, OA decreased UPRmt activation in fat-7(RNAi) and the empty vector (EV) control, LA decreased UPRmt in the EV control, and ARA supplementation decreased UPRmt in fat-6(RNAi). Supplementation with OA, LA, or ARA had no effect on basal UPRmt expression.

Supplementation of media with OA, LA, or ARA had no apparent effect on spg-7(RNAi)-mediated induction of the MAPKmt reporter (Fig S5–S7). In contrast, RNAi-mediated upregulation of the MAPKmt reporter under basal conditions was attenuated in fat-6 or fat-7 RNAis supplemented with OA and EV or mdt-15 RNAis supplemented with ARA. Supplementation with LA did not have overt effects on basal expression of the MAPKmt reporter. Finally, the expression of UPRmt and MAPKmt effector genes was examined in the presence of ARA under rotenone-induced mitochondrial stress (Fig. S8). ARA supplementation either decreased or did not affect UPRmt and MAPKmt effector gene expression, further supporting that fatty acid-mediated rescue is specific for the ESRE pathway.

sec.title: MDT-15/MED15 and FAT-6 are required for host defense against Pseudomonas aeruginosa in liquid pathogenesis model
sec.p: Mitochondrial ESRE surveillance is activated in response to liquid-based P. aeruginosa pathogenesis (LK-Pa) (10). Since MDT-15, FAT-6, and FAT-7 play a role in mitochondrial ESRE activation, their effect on host defense was measured. glp-4(bn2) worms were reared on mdt-15(RNAi), fat-6(RNAi), or fat-7(RNAi) and then examined in LK-Pa. Worms reared on mdt-15(RNAi) and fat-6(RNAi) from the L1 (Fig. S9A) or the L3 stage both showed increased sensitivity to LK-Pa (Fig. 6A).

To rule out the possibility that this effect was non-specific, glp-4(bn2) worms reared on RNAi targeting mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 were tested for sensitivity to Liquid Killing conditions where E. coli was substituted for P. aeruginosa (LK-Ec). Worms reared on RNAi from the L3 stage and exposed to LK-Ec (Fig. 6B) were indistinguishable from wild-type worms, suggesting that the sensitivity was specific to LK-Pa. As RNAi of these genes causes developmental changes (i.e., RNAi-treated worms are smaller in length and width), future experiments using RNAi feeding were performed starting at the L3 stage to limit these effects.

Previously, Pukkila-Worley et al. demonstrated that mdt-15 is involved in the regulation of innate immune activation and detoxification stress response (37). Using RNAi and an irg-4::GFP transcriptional reporter, they screened for candidate genes involved in the regulation of PMK-1/p38-dependent gene expression. mdt-15 was a hit in this screen, and gene expression analyses showed that mdt-15(RNAi) reduced expression of several PMK-1/p38-dependent genes, including genes important for resistance to P. aeruginosa.

However, it is unlikely that MDT-15 is regulating innate immune responses with the same mechanisms in Liquid and Slow Killing assays; previous research indicated that innate immune responses defending the host against P. aeruginosa in these two assays don’t generally overlap (10). For example, PMK-1/p38 MAPK is critical for resistance in Slow Killing assays (38,39) but PMK-1 activity is detrimental in Liquid Killing (40). Similarly, box C/D snoRNP genes promoting C. elegans survival in Slow Killing reduce survival in liquid-based pathogenesis (24,41).

To address this, we tested the effect of RNAi targeting mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 with RNAi starting at L3 stage (Fig. 6C)) on host defense against agar-based P. aeruginosa (SK-Pa) pathogenesis. Only mdt-15(RNAi) increased susceptibility to killing by P. aeruginosa in the agar pathogenesis model. The fact that MDT-15 is required for survival in both LK-Pa and SK-Pa is likely due to its critical role as a regulator of multiple pathways, including ESRE and the PMK-1/p38 pathway, which are crucial for host defense in LK-Pa and SK-Pa, respectively (10,37).

sec.title: Oleic acid promotes innate immune activation through induction of PMK-1/p38 pathway effectors
sec.p: As ESRE activation was observed in fat-6 and fat-7 RNAis in the presence of exogenous OA, we hypothesized that this OA-mediated ESRE activation would increase the survival of mdt-15 or fat-6 RNAis in LK-Pa. To test this, worms were fed fat-6 or mdt-15 RNAis and reared on agar plates with or without 500 μM OA supplementation. At the young adult stage, worms were tested in LK-Pa assays and death was measured at different timepoints (Fig. 6D).

Unexpectedly, OA supplementation led to higher, rather than reduced, susceptibility to killing by LK-Pa in EV and mdt-15 RNAis. The likeliest explanation for this is that OA supplementation triggered activation of the PMK-1/p38 MAPK pathway (13), which is known to reduce survival in Liquid Killing (40). To test this, PMK-1-dependent gene expression was evaluated in mdt-15(RNAi) or EV control worms with or without OA in the LK-Pa assay (Fig. 6E). Consistent with results from Pukkila-Worley et al., RNAi targeting mdt-15 decreased expression of PMK-1/p38 effectors. However, OA supplementation rescued expression of these immune effectors, even in LK-Pa conditions, where this pathway is normally inactive.

Since OA supplementation increased killing in EV and mdt-15(RNAi) during LK-Pa due to increased PMK-1-dependent gene expression, we hypothesized that downstream fatty acids would elicit similar results. To test this, we reared L3-stage worms on mdt-15(RNAi) or fat-6(RNAi) supplemented with 500 μM LA, 500 μM GLA, or 500 μM ARA. At the young adult stage, worms were tested in LK-Pa and death was measured. Supplementation with these fatty acids increased death by LK-Pa in worms treated with the EV control (Fig. S9B). qRT-PCR was used to measure PMK-1/p38 targets in worms fed with EV under basal conditions after supplementation with ARA (Fig. 6F). Similarly to OA, supplementation with ARA upregulated basal expression of PMK-1-dependent genes. Interestingly, when fatty acids were supplemented to EV, mdt-15, and fat-6 RNAis and treated with LK-Ec, we saw an increase in mdt-15 survival (Fig. S9C). This can be explained by the fact that PMK-1/p38 activation is not detrimental in the LK-Ec model as there is no pathogenesis that places additional burden onto host metabolism.

sec.title: Small nucleolar ribonucleoproteins play a role in innate immunity and mitochondrial surveillance through fatty acids
sec.p: Previously, Tjahjono et al. described a non-canonical role for box C/D small nucleolar ribonucleoproteins (snoRNPs) in mitochondrial surveillance and innate immunity. Box C/D snoRNPs FIB-1, NOL-56, and NOL-58 act as a molecular switch that turns on mitochondrial surveillance (ESRE and UPRmt) whilst repressing the PMK-1/p38 innate immune pathway (24). Given that OA is required for ESRE activation and snoRNPs are involved in mitochondrial surveillance, including ESRE activation, we tested the effect of exogenous OA supplementation on snoRNP knockdowns. We supplemented fib-1, nol-56, and nol-58 RNAis with 500μM OA on agar as well as 300 μM OA in the liquid treatment and observed the impact on rotenone-induced ESRE activation. Our results indicate that OA partially rescues ESRE activation in all snoRNP knockdowns (Fig. 7A–C), similar to its effect on knockdowns of fat-6 and fat-7. This suggests that OA may function downstream or in parallel to snoRNPs pathway. Additionally, we supplemented linoleic acid (LA), γ-linolenic acid (GLA), and arachidonic acid (ARA) as previously described to fib-1(RNAi) to evaluate their effect on ESRE activation. Our results show that these supplementations all rescued ESRE activation (Fig. 7D–F).

Tjahjono et al. had shown that box C/D snoRNPs are required for activation of mitochondrial surveillance (24). We decided to evaluate the effect of OA, LA, and ARA supplementation on UPRmt and MAPKmt activation in fib-1(RNAi) (Fig. S10A–B). Our results show that, in this background, supplementation of OA and LA decreased spg-7(RNAi)-induced UPRmt activation in the EV control while ARA did not elicit any effect (Fig. S10C). Additionally, supplementation of OA, LA, and ARA did not affect spg-7(RNAi)-induced MAPKmt activation (Fig. S10D) or basal expression of UPRmt nor MAPKmt (Fig. S10E–F). Altogether, these results strongly indicate a specific relationship between fatty acids, snoRNPs, and mitochondrial ESRE activation.

sec.title: The stearoyl-coA desaturase FAT-7 is expressed in response to mitochondrial damage and P. aeruginosa infection
sec.p: To assess whether fatty acid metabolism is affected under ESRE-inducing conditions, we used a worm strain (DMS303) carrying fat-7p::FAT-7::GFP multicopy array (42). We treated FAT-7::GFP worms with LK-Pa, SK-Pa, and their non-pathogenic counterparts, LK-Ec and SK-Ec, for 24 hours and then imaged after washing bacteria off. Our results show that FAT-7 is strongly upregulated in response to P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis, but not in response to agar-based pathogenesis (Fig. 8A–B). Additionally, we looked at the effect of mdt-15(RNAi) on FAT-7 activation in the LK-Pa assay and saw that knockdown of mdt-15 abolished FAT-7::GFP levels, consistent with the role of MDT-15 in the regulation of fatty acid metabolism (Fig. 8C–D).

To analyze the relationship between box C/D snoRNPs and fatty acid metabolism in ESRE regulation, we assessed FAT-7::GFP levels in RNAi knockdowns of fib-1, nol-56, and nol-58 in the LK-Pa assay. Interestingly, we saw that these RNAis led to significantly decreased FAT-7::GFP levels, revealing an unexpected connection between these pathways and indicating that snoRNPs function upstream of FAT-7 in ESRE regulation (Fig. 8E–F). Furthermore, treatment with mitochondrial stress inducer (40,43) iron chelator 1,10-phenanthroline also resulted in high levels of FAT-7::GFP (Fig. 8G–H), strengthening the connection between mitochondrial surveillance and fatty acid metabolism.

To gain insight into the regulatory mechanism for FAT-7 by box C/D snoRNPs, we conducted qRT-PCR to look at fat-7 expression under fib-1(RNAi) in ESRE-activating conditions. In contrast with translational reporter results, fat-7 expression is upregulated upon fib-1(RNAi) (Fig. 8I). These results suggest that box C/D snoRNPs regulate FAT-7 on the translational level.

Since OA rescued ESRE activation in fat-7 and box C/D snoRNPs RNAis and it promoted PMK-1/p38 effector upregulation, we tested the effect of OA supplementation on FAT-7::GFP activation under fib-1, nol-56, and nol-58 knockdowns in the LK-Pa assay. Our results show that OA supplementation is not sufficient to rescue FAT-7::GFP activation in these knockdowns (Fig. S11).

Since our results showed that box C/D snoRNPs regulate ESRE pathway through FAT-7, we were interested in studying the relationship between lipid metabolism and box H/ACA snoRNPs. To test this, we assessed phenanthroline-induced FAT-7::GFP levels in RNAis targeting nola-3/Nop10, Y48A6B.3/Nhp2, K015.5/dyskerin or Y66H1A.4/Gar1. Our results indicate that Y48A6B.3/Nhp2 and nola-3/Nop10 knockdowns reduce FAT-7::GFP levels (Fig. 8J–K), albeit to a lesser extent than box C/D snoRNPs, suggesting that box H/ACA snoRNPs are less directly involved with the regulation of FAT-7.

sec.title: Nuclear localization of FIB-1 is dependent on MDT-15
sec.p: To uncover the connection between box C/D snoRNPs, FAT-7, and fatty acid metabolism, we used a worm strain (COP262) carrying fib-1p::FIB-1(genomic)::eGFP::fib-1 3’ UTR single copy insertion (44). We reared FIB-1::GFP worms on EV, mdt-15, and fib-1 RNAis and examined their effect on basal FIB-1 expression. Our results indicate that mdt-15(RNAi) reduced the area of FIB-1 nuclei, uncovering an unanticipated regulatory mechanism of fatty acid metabolism for box C/D snoRNPs (Fig. 9A).

Since fatty acids downstream of FAT-6/FAT-7 were able to rescue ESRE activation in mdt-15, fat-6, and fib-1 knockdowns, we hypothesized that supplementation of the most downstream fatty acid could rescue FIB-1 expression in mdt-15(RNAi). To test this, we fed FIB-1::GFP worms EV or mdt-15 RNAis reared on either non-supplemented or 500 μM ARA-supplemented media. Non-supplemented worms were then treated with DMSO, and supplemented worms were treated with either DMSO or DMSO + 300 μM ARA. Our results show that fatty acid supplementation did not increase the level of nuclear FIB-1::GFP, suggesting that MDT-15 regulates it through a different mechanism. (Fig. 9B).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study we found that MDT-15/MED15 and box C/D snoRNPs work together to modulate fatty acid metabolism and mitochondrial surveillance activation, likely via regulation of fatty acid metabolism. We also found that MDT-15 and downstream effectors are required for host defense against P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis. Using bioinformatics approaches (i.e. Wormcat, RSAT, and MEME SUITE) on immune-stimulating compound LK56, we found an enrichment of differentially upregulated lipid metabolism genes, which also contained the ESRE motif in their promoter sequences (31–33). Similar results were observed when we looked at differentially upregulated genes in response to P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis, indicating a strong connection between lipid metabolism and host defense. By using worm reporters, we demonstrate the involvement of MDT-15/MED15, FAT-6/SCD, and FAT-7/SCD in the regulation of mitochondrial ESRE, UPRmt, and MAPKmt pathways, strengthening the link between lipid metabolism in the regulation of mitochondrial surveillance. This was an interesting finding as the role of lipids, such as ceramide, in the regulation of mitophagy and activation of UPRmt during proteotoxic stress was established (14,45,46), but this area of regulation remained unknown in mitochondrial ESRE.

Using cell biology assays, we were able to determine that fatty acids from C. elegans’ PUFA pathway play a vital role in mitochondrial ESRE activation, but not UPRmt or MAPKmt. Since MDT-15, FAT-6, and FAT-7 are required for UPRmt and/or MAPKmt, we hypothesized that exogenous supplementation of downstream fatty acids would be able to rescue activation under these RNAis as well. However, our results showed that supplementation failed to rescue UPRmt and MAPKmt activation, indicating this rescue is specific to ESRE. The most likely explanation for this is that ESRE exhibits a stronger sensitivity to ROS than UPRmt or MAPKmt (7). It has been shown that increased levels of long-chain fatty acids can affect mitochondrial redox activity and enhance the production of ROS at steady state respiration (47,48). This effect would be exacerbated during mitochondrial stress conditions, such as inhibition of Complex I by rotenone (49). With this in mind, supplementation of OA, LA, GLA, or ARA likely increases mitochondrial ROS production, which can induce ESRE activation even if there are low levels of MDT-15, FAT-6, and FAT-7. Additionally, when we supplemented these fatty acids, we saw earlier activation of 3XESRE::GFP by rotenone in our empty vector RNAi control, suggesting they could be inducing ROS, which in turn indirectly activates ESRE. Further experiments will need to be conducted to determine the exact mechanism by which these fatty acids rescue and promote early ESRE activation.

Given that various ESRE effectors are upregulated in response to P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis, we tested the effect of mdt-15, fat-6, and fat-7 RNAis on host defense in this context and observed their requirement for host survival. Additionally, we recapitulate published results by showing that mdt-15 is required for survival in P. aeruginosa agar-based pathogenesis (37). It is important to note that FAT-6 and FAT-7 have very similar roles in lipid metabolism, which often results in redundancy (50,51). In our case, fat-6 RNAi showed a stronger phenotype than fat-7 in the context of LK-Pa. Since our results showed rescue of ESRE activation by supplementation of OA, LA, GLA, and ARA, we hypothesized that this rescue in ESRE activation would be sufficient to promote host survival in LK-Pa. Our results showed the opposite effect, with higher death in supplemented worms, indicating our initial hypothesis was wrong. Published research has shown that stearidonic acid (SDA) and GLA are required for activation of PMK-1-dependent innate immune effectors (52). Similarly, we hypothesized that supplementation of OA, LA, GLA, and ARA could activate PMK-1-dependent innate immune effectors and lead to higher death in LK-Pa. Our results indicate that OA and the most downstream fatty acid, ARA, can induce PMK-1-dependent gene expression, providing an explanation for why supplemented worms died more than the non-supplemented cohort. Activation of PMK-1-dependent effectors leads to higher susceptibility to killing in LK-Pa likely because these demand important resources, such as chaperones, that would also be required for mitigating organellular damage elicited by pyoverdine (40,53).

In this study, we also explored the relationship between box C/D snoRNPs, mitochondrial surveillance, and lipid metabolism. Published research has demonstrated non-canonical roles for box C/D snoRNPs in the context of mitochondrial surveillance and innate immunity (21,23,24). Since fatty acids are vital for ESRE activation, as well as box C/D snoRNPs, we evaluated the effect of supplementation on RNAi of these components. Our results demonstrate that fatty acids can rescue ESRE activation in fib-1 RNAi, an important component of box C/D snoRNPs. Similarly to mdt-15, fat-6, and fat-7 RNAis, supplementation of these fats to fib-1 RNAi did not rescue activation of UPRmt nor MAPKmt pathways, indicating again this is specific for ESRE pathway. Our results provide a novel link between lipid metabolism and box C/D snoRNPs in the context of mitochondrial surveillance.

To explore this connection, we used worm reporters to conduct epistatic experiments for box C/D snoRNPs and lipid metabolism. Our results indicate that box C/D snoRNPs regulate expression of FAT-7 during P. aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis and mitochondrial damage. Furthermore, supplementation of exogenous OA does not rescue FAT-7 expression in mdt-15 nor fib-1 RNAi, indicating fatty acids are not a required component for proper fat-7 expression and/or translation. Previous studies have elucidated regulatory mechanisms for box C/D snoRNPs in the context of nucleolar localization (54,55). Our results indicate that basal FIB-1 expression is MDT-15-dependent, providing a novel regulatory mechanism for box C/D snoRNPs.

Investigation into the regulatory pathways involving box C/D snoRNPs may provide insight into the pathologies of various cancers that have been associated with misexpression of box C/D snoRNAs. Here, we focus on the regulation of lipid metabolism by box C/D snoRNPs, but snoRNPs are implicated in other pathways such as the PI3K/AKR/mTOR, MAPK, and p53-dependent apoptosis pathway, which if dysregulated, may result in apoptosis inhibition, increased proliferation, and epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT); factors that are associated with tumor development (56). Additionally, several box C/D snoRNAs have been identified to be diagnostic and prognostic markers of cancer progression and could serve as potential cancer treatment targets (57). Our research serves as a starting point for investigating the regulation of snoRNPs, potentially leading to therapies targeting tumor progression.

sec.title: C. elegans Strains
sec.p: All C. elegans strains were maintained on nematode growth medium (NGM) plates seeded with Escherichia coli strain OP50 as the food source and were maintained at 22°C (58), unless noted otherwise. C. elegans strains used in this study included N2 Bristol (wild-type) (59) , SS104 (temperature-sensitive mutant that produces progeny at 15°C and is sterile at 25°C ) |glp-4(bn2)I| (60), WY703 |fdIs2 [3XESRE::GFP]; pFF4[rol-6(su1006)]| (61), SJ4100 |zcIs13 [Phsp-6::GFP]| (62), SLR115 |dvIs67 [Ptbb-6::GFP + Pmyo-3::dsRed]| (6), DMS303 |nIs590 [fat-7p::FAT-7::GFP + lin15(+)] V| (42), and COP262 |knuSi221[fib-1p::FIB-1(genomic)::eGFP::fib-1 3’ UTR + unc-119(+)]| (44).

Synchronized worms were prepared by hypochlorite isolation of eggs from gravid adults, followed by hatching of the eggs in pure S Basal. 6,000 synchronized L1 larvae were transferred onto 10 cm NGM plates seeded with OP50. After transfer, worms were grown at 22°C for 48 hours before experiments, or for three days for the next isolation of eggs. For synchronization of SS104 worms, 6,000 synchronized L1 larvae were transferred onto 10 cm NGM plates seeded with OP50 and grown at 15°C for five days for the next isolation of eggs. L4 hermaphrodite worms were used for all assays unless specified in the text.

sec.title: Bacterial strains
sec.p: RNAi experiments in this study were done using RNAi-competent E. coli (HT115-based) obtained from the Ahringer or Vidal RNAi libraries (63,64). All strains were sequenced prior to use. For experiments involving P. aeruginosa, the PA14 strain was used (65).

sec.title: RNA interference protocol
sec.p: RNAi-expressing E. coli were cultured and seeded onto NGM plates supplemented with 25 μg/mL carbenicillin and 1 mM IPTG. For RNAi experiments starting at L1, 1500 synchronized L1 larvae were transferred onto 6 cm RNAi plates and grown at 22°C for 48 hours prior to imaging, exposure to chemical treatment or pathogens. For double RNAi experiments, specifically pertaining to Phsp-6::GFP and Ptbb-6::GFP experiments, RNAi was mixed at a 3:1 ratio with spg-7(RNAi) or EV control respectively.

For RNAi experiments starting at L3, 6,000 synchronized L1 larvae were transferred onto 10 cm regular NGM plates seeded with OP50 and grown at 22°C for 22 hours until reaching the L3 stage. Worms were then washed off the plates, rinsed three times with S Basal, and transferred onto RNAi plates. These worms were then grown at 22°C for 24 hours on RNAi plates before use for experiments.

sec.title: C. elegans chemical exposure assays
sec.p: Synchronized L4-stage worms were washed from NGM plates seeded with E. coli OP50, HT115-based RNAi-expressing E. coli, or OP50-based RNAi-expressing E. coli into a 15 mL conical tube and rinsed three times. For experiments involving the use of 3XESRE::GFP strain, worms were sorted into a 96-well half-area plate (~100 worms/well), and the total volume per well, including treatment, was 100 μL. Treatment with rotenone (Sigma) was done at a final concentration of 25 μM per well. For liquid supplementation of fatty acids, a final concentration of 300 μM per well was used. For treatment with 1,10-Phenanthroline, a final concentration of 4 mM per well was used. DMSO was used as the solvent control for all of these treatments at a v/v ratio relative to the treatment.

For experiments involving the use of the FAT-7::GFP strain, worms were sorted into a 96-well full area plate (~150 worms/well) and a total volume per well of 160 μL including treatment. Worms were imaged with Cytation5 automated microscope every hour for eighteen hours at room temperature. At least three biological replicates were performed for each experiment.

sec.title: C. elegans fatty acid supplementation assays
sec.p: Fatty acids were obtained from Nu-Chek-Prep, Inc. and were prepared as previously described (66). Assays were performed by growing synchronized L1 worms on the indicated fatty acid or control media. Unless otherwise indicated, OA, LA, GLA, and ARA were used at a final concentration of 500 μM. Fatty acid supplementation assays recommend the addition of a surfactant, such as NP-40 (Tergitol), to the agar prior to autoclaving in order to increase the solubility of the fatty acid in the media. However, the fatty acid can also be delivered via DMSO as its vehicle. We got the same results on fatty acid supplementation experiments with the use of NP-40 and using DMSO as our vehicle for fatty acid supplementation (Fig. S2).

sec.title: Quantitative reverse transcriptase PCR (qRT-PCR)
sec.p: 30,000 young adult worms were used for RNA extraction, and subsequent qRT-PCR was performed as previously described (67). Before RNA extraction, worms were reared on RNAi plates starting at the L1 stage, as previously described. For treatment with rotenone, L4-stage worms were washed off plates, rinsed three times, and were incubated for 8 hours in S Basal supplemented with 25 μM rotenone, 300uM fatty acid + 25uM rotenone, or a corresponding volume of DMSO control. For LK-Pa treatment, young adult worms were washed off plates, rinsed three times, and were sorted onto a 6 well plate (~10,000 worms/mL per well). Then, 2mL of LK medium mixed with P. aeruginosa PA14 were added to each well for a final volume of 3mLs and a final OD600 of 0.03. For each experiment, at least three biological replicates were performed.

sec.title: C. elegans pathogenesis assays
sec.p: P. aeruginosa liquid pathogenesis model (Liquid Killing or LK-Pa) was performed essentially as described (67,68). 25 synchronized young adult glp-4 mutants were sorted into each well of a 384-well plate. LK medium was mixed with P. aeruginosa PA14 (final OD600: 0.03) and then added into each well. Plates were incubated at 25 °C. At different time points, plates were washed three times and worms were stained with SYTOX Orange nucleic acid stain (Invitrogen) for 12 hours to stain dead worms. Afterwards, plates were washed and then imaged using a Cytation5 automated microscope, and the fraction of dead worms was quantified autonomously with CellProfiler software. P. aeruginosa agar pathogenesis model (Slow Killing or SK-Pa) was performed as previously described (69,70). 50 young adult worms were transferred onto SK-Pa plates and incubated at 25 °C. Worms were scored daily to obtain survival curves; dead worms were extracted from assay plates and worms that left the surface of the plate were censored from data analysis. P. aeruginosa liquid-based exposure to worms carrying fat-7p::FAT-7::GFP reporter (DMS303) was performed similarly to the LK-Pa assay. About 150 worms were sorted into each well of a 96-well full-area plate. Liquid Killing medium was mixed with P. aeruginosa PA14 (final OD600: 0.03) and then added into each well for a total volume of 160 uL. After 24 hours, bacteria were washed off the plates, and worms were imaged. Imaging and GFP quantification were performed with the Cytation5 automated microscope and Gen5 3.0 software.

sec.title: Microscopy
sec.p: For visualization of COP262 (FIB-1::GFP), worms were immobilized using 10mM levamisole and transferred onto a 3% noble agar slide. At least three biological replicates with 15 worms per replicate were imaged using a Zeiss ApoTome.2 Imager.M2, Carl Zeiss, Germany with 63x magnification. Hypodermic nuclei in the tail of each worm were imaged and quantified using the 10.2.0 version Imaris software.

sec.title: Statistical Analyses
sec.p: RStudio (version 4.3.0) was used to perform statistical analysis. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to calculate the significance of a treatment or a condition when there were more than three cohorts in the experimental setting. Statistically significant results as determined via ANOVA were then followed by Dunnett’s test (R package DescTools, version 0.99.34) to calculate statistical significance or p values between each group compared to the control group. Dunnett’s test was indicated in graphs as follows: no star = not significant, *p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001.

caption.p: Figure S1. Changes in ESRE reporter expression upon mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAis are more robust than those of UPRmt and MAPKmt. (A) Bar graph depicting changes in reporter expression of worms reared on mdt-15, fat-6, and fat-7 RNAis. Initial quantifications for percent GFP area of worms reared on EV RNAi for each reporter were normalized to 100, and mdt-15, fat-6, and fat-7 RNAi were normalized to their own EV. Relative reporter expression depicts the normalized values of percent GFP area for each RNAi relative to their EV in each reporter strain. Red stars indicate comparisons between reporters for each RNAi. Statistical significance for reporter activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05.

Figure S2. Exogenous supplementation of oleic acid using NP-40 as the vehicle elicits same results as using DMSO as the vehicle. (A) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV or fat-7 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone at the L4 stage. Worms were supplemented with 500μM OA on agar and 300μM OA in liquid in the presence of 0.1% NP-40 (Tergitol). (B) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE activation. (C) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 14 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with OA. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparisons between OA supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparisons between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

Figure S3. In the absence of mitochondrial damage, oleic acid is not sufficient to trigger ESRE activation. (A) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE activation. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparisons between OA supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparisons between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

Figure S4. Linoleic acid and gamma-linolenic acid are required for ESRE activation. (A, D) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone at the L4 stage. Worms supplemented with 500μM LA on agar and 300μM LA in liquid (A) or with GLA in a similar way (D). (B, E) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout on ESRE activation. (C, F) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 15 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with LA or GLA. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

Figure S5. Oleic acid supplementation does not rescue UPRmt or MAPKmt activation in mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAi. (A) Fluorescent images of Phsp-6::GFP-carrying worms or Ptbb-6::GFP-carrying worms (B) reared on EV or fat-6 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAis. Worms were supplemented with 500μM OA on agar. (C-F) Quantification of percent GFP area under specified condition, which gives a readout of reporter activation. Statistical significance for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

Figure S6. Linoleic acid supplementation does not rescue UPRmt or MAPKmt activation in mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAis. (A) Fluorescent images of Phsp-6::GFP-carrying worms or Ptbb-6::GFP-carrying worms (B) reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAis. Worms were supplemented with 500μM LA on agar. (C-F) Quantification of percent GFP area under specified condition, which gives a readout of reporter activation. Statistical significance for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

Figure S7. Arachidonic acid supplementation does not rescue UPRmt or MAPKmt activation in mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAis. (A) Fluorescent images of Phsp-6::GFP-carrying worms or Ptbb-6::GFP-carrying worms (B) reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAis. Worms were supplemented with 500μM ARA on agar. (C-F) Quantification of percent GFP area under specified condition, which gives a readout of reporter activation. Statistical significance for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

Figure S8. Arachidonic acid supplementation decreases UPRmt and MAPKmt gene expression. (A) Expression levels of native UPRmt or MAPKmt genes in 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV RNAi (starting at L1 stage) and treated for 8 hours with 25μM rotenone, 300uM ARA + 25uM rotenone, or with corresponding volumes of DMSO at L4 stage. Fold changes were normalized to their own DMSO-treated cohort. Statistical significance for gene expression was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between ARA supplementation and no supplementation for each gene.

Figure S9. MDT-15 and its downstream effectors are required for host defense. (A) glp-4(bn2) worms reared at the L1 stage on EV, mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAis. At the young adult stage, they were treated with LK-Pa (B-C) glp-4(bn2) worms reared on EV, mdt-15 and fat-6 RNAis supplemented with 500 μM OA, LA, GLA, or ARA at the L3 stage. At the young adult stage, they were treated with LK-Pa or LK-Ec (C). Statistical significance for percent death was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

Figure S10. Fatty acid supplementation does not rescue UPRmt or MAPKmt activation in fib-1 RNAi. (A) Fluorescent images of Phsp-6::GFP-carrying worms or Ptbb-6::GFP-carrying worms (B) reared on EV or fib-1 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAis. Worms were supplemented with 500μM ARA on agar. (C-F) Quantification of percent GFP area under specified condition, which gives a readout of reporter activation. Statistical significance for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

Figure S11. Oleic acid does not rescue FAT-7 activation in mdt-15 and box C/D snoRNPs knockdowns. (A) FAT-7::GFP worms reared on EV, mdt-15, fib-1, nol-56, or nol-58 RNAis supplemented with 500μM OA. Worms were treated at the L4 stage with LK-Pa for 24 hours before imaging. Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout on reporter activation. Statistical significance for FAT-7 activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between OA supplementation and no supplementation. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

caption.title: Bioinformatic analyses reveal a potential link between lipid metabolism, host defense, and mitochondrial surveillance.
caption.p: (A-C) De novo motif identified in a subset of genes upregulated in response to 7% ethanol exposure (A), liquid-based P. aeruginosa pathogenesis (B), or treatment with immune-stimulating compound, LK56 (C). (D) Gene ontology analyses (Wormcat) on genes upregulated in response to LK56 treatment. Horizontal bargraph depicts categories significantly enriched (p ≥ 0.05) and p-values were adjusted by taking the negative logarithm base 10 of original p-value. (E) Fluorescent images of C. elegans, strain WY703, carrying a 3XESRE::GFP reporter reared on HT115-based empty vector (EV) RNAi or HT115-based mdt-15 RNAi and treated with 25μM rotenone (F) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout on the percent of ESRE activation per well. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

caption.title: The mediator subunit MDT-15 and its downstream effectors fat-6 and fat-7 are required for ESRE activation.
caption.p: (A) Diagram depicting a section of C. elegans’ polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis (PUFA) pathway where stearic acid (SA) is converted to oleic acid (OA) by the action of desaturases FAT-6/FAT-7, which are known to be regulated by MDT-15. It is also noted from previous research by Anderson et al. that OA is required for irg-4::GFP activation. (B) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV or fat-6 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone. (C) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE activation. (D) Expression level of native ESRE genes in 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV, mdt-15, or fat-6 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone or v/v DMSO. Worms were reared on RNAi at the L1 stage and treated with rotenone or DMSO for 8 hours at the L4 stage. Gene expression levels were normalized to their own DMSO-treated cohort. Statistical significance for ESRE activation and gene expression was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

caption.title: UPRmt and MAPKmt activation partially require MDT-15, FAT-6, and FAT-7.
caption.p: (A) Fluorescent images of Phsp-6::GFP reporter-carrying worms reared on EV, mdt-15, or atfs-1 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAis. (B) Fluorescent images of Ptbb-6::GFP reporter-carrying worms reared on HT115-based EV, mdt-15, or pmk-3 RNAis. Each RNAi was mixed in a 3:1 ratio with spg-7 or EV RNAi. (C-F) Quantification of percent GFP area under specified condition, which gives a readout of reporter activation. Blue bars represent positive control and dark gray bars represent negative control. Statistical significance for UPRmt or MAPKmt activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

caption.title: The monounsaturated oleic acid is required for ESRE activation.
caption.p: (A) Diagram depicting a section of C. elegans’ polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis (PUFA) pathway where stearic acid (SA) is converted to oleic acid (OA) by the action of desaturases FAT-6/FAT-7, which are known to be regulated by MDT-15. It is also noted from previous research by Anderson et al. that OA is required for irg-4::GFP activation. Diagram also shows that OA is required for 3XESRE::GFP (ESRE) activation. (B) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 14 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with OA. (C) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV or fat-6 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone at the L4 stage. Worms were supplemented with 500μM OA on agar and 300μM OA in liquid. (D) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparisons between OA supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparisons between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

caption.title: The most downstream fatty acid in C. elegans’ PUFA pathway, arachidonic acid, is required for mitochondrial ESRE activation.
caption.p: (A) Diagram depicting a larger section of C. elegans’ polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis (PUFA) pathway, showing fatty acids further downstream of oleic acid. (B) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone at the L4 stage. Worms were supplemented with 500μM ARA on agar and 300μM ARA in liquid. (C) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE. (D) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 15 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with ARA. (E) Expression level of native ESRE genes in WY703 worms reared on EV RNAi (starting at L1 stage) and treated for 8 hours with 25μM rotenone, 300uM ARA + 25uM rotenone, or with corresponding volumes of DMSO at L4 stage. Fold changes were normalized to their own DMSO-treated cohort. Statistical significance for ESRE activation and gene expression was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between ARA supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi or gene. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

caption.title: Lipid metabolism plays a role in host defense.
caption.p: (A-B) glp-4(bn2) worms reared at the L3 stage on EV, mdt-15, fat-6, or fat-7 RNAis. At the young adult stage, they were treated with LK-Pa (A) or LK-Ec (B). Dark grey bars represent the EV RNAi control (C) Worms were reared as above and transferred to slow-killing plates spread with P. aeruginosa at the young adult stage. Worms were scored daily to obtain percent death. Day on which death in SK-Pa was similar to LK-Pa for EV(RNAi) is shown. Dark grey bars represent the EV RNAi control. (D) glp-4(bn2) worms were reared on EV and fat-6 RNAis supplemented with 500 μM OA at the L1 stage or on EV and mdt-15 RNAis supplemented with 500 μM OA at the L3-stage. At the young adult stage, they were treated with LK-Pa. Blue bars represent the non-supplemented cohort and yellow bars represent OA-supplemented cohort. (E) Expression levels of PMK-1-dependent genes in glp-4(bn2) worms reared on mdt-15 RNAi supplemented with 500 μM OA at the L1 stage and treated with LK-Pa at the young adult stage for 24h. Gene expression levels were normalized to their own non-supplemented cohort. (F) Basal expression level of PMK-1-dependent genes in 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV RNAi supplemented with 500 μM ARA at the L1 stage. Worms were treated with DMSO at the L4 stage for 8h. Gene expression levels were normalized to their own non-supplemented cohort. Statistical significance for percent death and gene expression was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation for each RNAi. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

caption.title: Fatty acid supplementation rescues ESRE activation in box C/D snoRNP RNAis.
caption.p: (A) Fluorescent images of 3XESRE::GFP worms reared on EV RNAi or nol-58 RNAi and treated with 25μM rotenone at the L4 stage. Worms were supplemented with 500μM OA on agar and 300μM OA in liquid. (B) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE activation. (C) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 14 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with OA. (D) Worms were prepared as in (A), only ARA was used instead of OA. (E) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of ESRE activation. Worms were reared on EV or fib-1 RNAis, and supplemented with LA, GLA, or ARA. (F) Curve representing ESRE activation by rotenone across 14 hours. Solid lines represent ESRE activation of non-supplemented worms. Dotted lines represent ESRE activation of worms supplemented with ARA. Statistical significance for ESRE activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between fatty acid supplementation and no supplementation. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.

caption.title: FAT-7 is expressed in response to LK-Pa and mitochondrial damage in an MDT-15 and box C/D snoRNPs-dependent manner.
caption.p: (A) Fluorescent images of FAT-7::GFP worms treated at the L4-stage with LK-Pa, LK-Ec, and SK-Pa for 24 hours before imaging. (B, D, F, H, K) Quantification of percent GFP area, which gives a readout of FAT-7 levels. (C, E) Fluorescent images FAT-7::GFP worms reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis (C) or fib-1(RNAi) (E). Worms were treated at the L4 stage with LK-Pa for 24 hours before imaging. (G) FAT-7::GFP worms were reared on EV, mdt-15, fat-6, or fib-1 RNAis. Worms were treated at the L4 stage with 4 mM 1,10-phenanthroline and v/v DMSO (solvent control). (I) Expression levels of native fat-7 gene in N2 worms reared on EV or fib-1 RNAis and treated with 25μM rotenone and v/v DMSO. Worms were reared on RNAi at the L1 stage and treated with rotenone or DMSO for 8 hours at the L4 stage. Gene expression levels were normalized to their DMSO cohort. (J) FAT-7::GFP worms were reared on EV, Y66H1A.4, Y48A6B.3, K01G5.5, or nola-3 RNAis. Worms were treated at the L4 stage with 4 mM 1,10-phenanthroline and v/v DMSO (solvent control). Statistical significance for FAT-7 activation was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV.

caption.title: MDT-15 regulates FIB-1 expression.
caption.p: (A) FIB-1::GFP reporter worms were reared on EV, mdt-15, or fib-1 RNAis. At the L4 stage, worms were treated with DMSO for 12 hours. Hypodermal nuclei in the tail of each worm were imaged, and the area of fluorescent puncta was quantified. (B) C. elegans FIB-1::GFP worms were reared on EV or mdt-15 RNAis supplemented with 500μM ARA on agar and 300μM ARA in liquid. At the L4 stage, worms were treated with DMSO for 12 hours. Hypodermal nuclei in the tail of each worm were imaged, and the area of fluorescent puncta was quantified. Statistical significance for FIB-1 expression was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s Test, *** p ≤ 0.001, ** p ≤ 0.01, * p ≤ 0.05, no stars if p > 0.05. In all panels, red stars indicate comparison between ARA supplementation and no supplementation. In all panels, black stars indicate comparison between RNAi and EV in the non-supplemented cohort.
